elements.sent-2411-001: Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) has been applied to more and more scenarios due to its excellent performance in many machine learning tasks, especially with deep and complex structures.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, as the network goes deeper, more parameters need to be stored and optimized.
elements.sent-2411-003: Besides, almost all common CNN models adopt ``train-and-use" strategy where the structure is pre-defined and the kernel parameters are fixed after the training with the same structure and set of parameters used for all data without considering the content complexity.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this paper, we propose a new CNN framework, named as Puppet-CNN, which contains two modules: a puppet module and a puppeteer module.
elements.sent-2411-005: The puppet module is a CNN model used to actually process the input data just like other works, but its depth and kernels are generated by the puppeteer module (realized with Ordinary Differential Equation (ODE)) based on the input complexity each time.
elements.sent-2411-006: By recurrently generating kernel parameters in the puppet module, we can take advantage of the dependence among kernels of different convolutional layers to significantly reduce the size of CNN model by only storing and training the parameters of the much smaller puppeteer ODE module.
elements.sent-2411-007: Through experiments on several datasets, our method has proven to be superior than the traditional CNNs on both performance and efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-008: The model size can be reduced more than 10 times.
elements.sent-2411-009: In recent years, Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) have become the most popular structure in deep learning community.
elements.sent-2411-010: As one of the key building blocks in artificial intelligence, CNNs are playing a critical role in many machine learning applications.
elements.sent-2411-011: Through unremitting exploration and improvement, CNNs have proven to be effective in many research tasks, where the model structures are often complex and deep.
elements.sent-2411-012: However, traditional CNN suffers from several limitations, which compromises its actual efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-013: First, although a wider and deeper network usually has better performance~\cite{He_2016_CVPR, NIPS2012_c399862d, 7780677 , it comes at the cost of an explosion in the number of network parameters and the consequent challenges in the model storage and optimization, which hinders its widespread adoption on many devices.}
elements.sent-2411-014: Second, CNN was designed to emulate the information process of the human brain on the neuron level with a serial of processing and interactions across convolutional layers.
elements.sent-2411-015: However, existing models are often constructed with independent neurons (i.e. convolutional kernels in CNNs), ignoring the inter-layer dependencies during the processing.
elements.sent-2411-016: Last, almost all classic CNNs adopt ``train-and-use" strategy.
elements.sent-2411-017: The structure of the network (such as depth) is pre-defined, the kernel parameters are fixed after the training, and the same set of kernels and the same network structure are applied to all data samples.
elements.sent-2411-018: However, different data samples contain contents of different complexities, and a network should ideally process every data sample with an appropriate structure and set of kernels to reach the best balance between the performance and efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-019: Obviously, current CNNs are not flexible enough to deal with such heterogeneity in data, although it is very common to see in the real world.
elements.sent-2411-020: To tackle the performance-efficiency problem, some efforts have been made on model compression, using techniques such as playing kernel tricks, parameter quantization and knowledge distillation.
elements.sent-2411-021: However, the reduction of computational complexity was usually at the cost of performance compromise.
elements.sent-2411-022: Also, all of them still tried to find a uniform network structure with the same set of parameters for all data although there is a difference in application requirements and data complexity.
elements.sent-2411-023: A deep fixed infrastructure trying to handling all data may be redundant and introduce unnecessary complicatedness, which may also compromise the efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-024: Some researchers~\cite{https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.1605.06431 found that not all data samples needed deep structures and proposed using CNNs with input-adaptive depth.}
elements.sent-2411-025: Nevertheless, no matter what the proposed strategy was based upon, early-exiting~\cite{10.5555/3305381.3305436, 10.5555/3304889.3304963, https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.1709.01686, https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.1706.00885, 2015arXiv151107838A, huang2018multiscale, Yang_2020_CVPR or layer-skipping~\cite{Wang_2018_ECCV, Veit2018, Figurnov_2017_CVPR, leroux2018iamnn:, 8954249, blockdrop , previous depth-adaptive CNNs always used the method of ``substraction", i.e., training a very large model and only executing a part of it each time.}}
elements.sent-2411-026: Also, for different data samples, only the network structure was adaptive but the model parameters could not be adjusted accordingly.
elements.sent-2411-027: On the other hand, parameter-adaptive networks~\cite{6796337, NEURIPS2019_f2201f51, Chen_2020_CVPR, debrabandere16dynamic, ha2017hypernetworks, inbook_LS_DFN, ma2020weightnet, Zhou_2021_CVPR applied an independent small network to generate input-adaptive parameters for a pre-defined network structure.}
elements.sent-2411-028: Although the training of a small network helps reduce the complexity faced by a deep network, the possible acceleration through the change of network structure is neglected.
elements.sent-2411-029: In this paper, we propose a new framework, named Puppet-CNN, to well balance the model performance and efficiency for CNNs.
elements.sent-2411-030: More specifically, our framework is composed of two modules: a puppet module to process input data like done by a common CNN and a puppeteer module.
elements.sent-2411-031: All kernels of the puppet module are generated by the puppeteer module, a continuous-time recurrent model constructed based on Ordinary Differential Equation (ODE).
elements.sent-2411-032: By doing so, the optimization of a very deep puppet network is translated to the optimization of the puppeteer module, which is much smaller with much fewer parameters to store.
elements.sent-2411-033: Besides, the kernels and depth of the puppet network are determined by the puppeteer ODE according to the complexity of the input data, which makes our framework more flexible and adaptive.
elements.sent-2411-034: Further, by using ODE to generate kernel parameters in CNN, the convolutional layers in the puppet module can be inter-dependent for better extracting sequential features across layers.
elements.sent-2411-035: We would like to mention that our framework is ``puppet-independent", that is, the puppet module can be any type of CNN model, no matter it is hand-crafted or generated through Network Architecture Search (NAS).
elements.sent-2411-036: The contributions of this paper lie in three folds:.
elements.sent-2411-037: We propose a new ``Puppeteer-Puppet" framework for CNN, where the kernel parameters of the puppet network are generated by the puppeteer module, such that a very deep network can be generated but the overall model size is much smaller;.
elements.sent-2411-038: The generated kernels and the depth of the puppet network are input-adaptive based on the content complexity of the data samples, so that the puppet network is most suitable for each data sample;.
elements.sent-2411-039: The puppeteer module and the puppet module are separable, which means the puppet module can be an arbitrary CNN and optimized through our puppeteer module, i.e. our framework is flexible enough to work with almost all existing CNN structures.
elements.sent-2411-040: The organization of the remainder of the paper is as follows: In Sec.~\ref{sec:2 , we give an overview of the related works.}
elements.sent-2411-041: We introduce our model in details in Sec.~\ref{sec:3 and demonstrate its effectiveness through experiments in Sec.~\ref{sec:4 .}}
elements.sent-2411-042: Finally, we conclude our work in Sec.~\ref{sec:5 .}
elements.sent-2411-043: As a previous consensus, neural networks would be more effective with the increase of depth and width~\cite{He_2016_CVPR, NIPS2012_c399862d, 7780677 .}
elements.sent-2411-044: However, deep structures are found to be unnecessary~\cite{https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.1605.06431 in many cases, especially when dealing with simple inputs.}
elements.sent-2411-045: Rather than using a fixed structure for all data samples, input-adaptive networks were considered, where simple inputs only go through a shallow structure and complex ones go through a deep model.
elements.sent-2411-046: In Early-Exiting~\cite{10.5555/3305381.3305436, 10.5555/3304889.3304963, https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.1709.01686, https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.1706.00885 methods, simple samples were allowed to exit the model earlier.}
elements.sent-2411-047: Generally, a model could use any existing CNN structures, such as in~\cite{https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.1709.01686, 10.5555/3305381.3305436 , or stack a set of them with an increased depth like done in~\cite{2015arXiv151107838A, chen2018biglittle, https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.1706.00885 .}}
elements.sent-2411-048: Criteria, such as the model performance achieved based on the features from previous layers~\cite{10.5555/3305381.3305436, 10.5555/3304889.3304963 or the uncertainty of the intermediate classifier output~\cite{https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.1709.01686, https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.1706.00885 , were applied to determine whether the Early-Exiting should be triggered.}}
elements.sent-2411-049: MSD-Net~\cite{huang2018multiscale integrated dense connection~\cite{Huang_2017_CVPR and multi-resolution branches, and the same idea was also used in~\cite{10.1145/3340531.3411973, Yang_2020_CVPR .}}}
elements.sent-2411-050: In~\cite{https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.1703.06217 , all inputs were forced to pass through shallow layers and changed were only made on the deep layers.}
elements.sent-2411-051: To make the network more adaptive,~Layer Skipping were not only performed on deep layers, but also anywhere according to the input.
elements.sent-2411-052: Gating functions were used in~\cite{Wang_2018_ECCV, Veit2018 , where the output from the previous layer was applied to determine whether the current layer should be skipped.}
elements.sent-2411-053: Built upon Adaptive Computation Time (ACT)~\cite{https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.1603.08983 , SACT-ResNet~\cite{Figurnov_2017_CVPR used a halting score to only keep part of layers within each residual block, while IAMNN~\cite{leroux2018iamnn: also enforced the parameter sharing among layers within the block.}}}
elements.sent-2411-054: Dynamic Recursive Neural Network (DRNN)~\cite{8954249 further extended the idea in~\cite{leroux2018iamnn: by replacing the original residual block with a recursive stack of the same residual layer.}}
elements.sent-2411-055: Our model also supports the use of a stack of residual layers within a block, but they are associated with slightly different parameters to make a block more expressive in capturing data features, and parameters between adjacent residual blocks are made related to better emulate the knowledge extraction process of a human brain.
elements.sent-2411-056: Although all these depth-adaptive methods utilized the input-adaptive strategy, they lacked the further parameter adjustment based on inputs, which might also affect the final performance.
elements.sent-2411-057: Besides, they all needed to train and store a large model during the training phase but only selected part of blocks or parameters in the testing phase.
elements.sent-2411-058: In most traditional CNN works, parameters are optimized over all the training data and then fixed and applied to all the inputs during the inference, which limits the learning and expressive capabilities of the networks.
elements.sent-2411-059: Inspired by ~\cite{6796337 , some attention has been drawn to use adaptive parameters, and methods are divided into two main branches: Parameter Adjustment and Parameter Generation.}
elements.sent-2411-060: With the first group, a fixed set of kernel parameters are still learned over the whole dataset, but the weights are adapted in response to the input during the testing phase using schemes such as an sample-wise attention mask in CondConv~\cite{NEURIPS2019_f2201f51 .}
elements.sent-2411-061: Dynamic Convolution~\cite{Chen_2020_CVPR further added a normalized constraint to the attention mask.}
elements.sent-2411-062: In~\cite{Harley_2017_ICCV, Su_2019_CVPR , spatial-specific soft attention was added to make the kernels dynamic.}
elements.sent-2411-063: Rather than adjusting model parameters through attention on the fly, Parameter Generation or Parameter Prediction~\cite{Denil2013PredictingPI directly generate parameters according to inputs using another model during testing, which is more straightforward but challenging.}
elements.sent-2411-064: In the early-stage work~\cite{Denil2013PredictingPI , the parameter matrix of the model was represented as a product of two smaller matrices, with one fixed after the training and the other obtained according to the input at the test time.}
elements.sent-2411-065: As two classic approaches of parameter generation, Dynamic Filter Network (DFN)~\cite{debrabandere16dynamic and HyperNetwork~\cite{ha2017hypernetworks generated parameters at run time with a separate network for CNN and RNN respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-066: As an extension from DFN, LS-DFN~\cite{inbook_LS_DFN generated spatial-specific parameters while the Decoupled Dynamic Filter (DDF) Network~\cite{Zhou_2021_CVPR decoupled dynamic kernels into spatial and channel ones.}}
elements.sent-2411-067: Meta Networks~\cite{https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.1703.00837 generated parameters using a meta learner across tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-068: Generalizing the scheme in~\cite{NEURIPS2019_f2201f51 , in WeightNet~\cite{ma2020weightnet , kernel parameters were generated through grouped FC layers.}}
elements.sent-2411-069: Besides, extra information, such as camera perspective, was integrated for parameter generation in~\cite{NIPS2017_e7e23670 , and edge attributes were utilized to generate convolutional parameters on graphs in~\cite{Simonovsky_2017_CVPR .}}
elements.sent-2411-070: Although parameters became input-adaptive in above works, the network structure was not adaptive correspondingly.
elements.sent-2411-071: Still using a common DNN or CNN, parameters generated for each convolutional layer were independent.
elements.sent-2411-072: As the feature map was used as the input to obtain the new parameters for the next layer, the parameter-generation network needed to be relatively large to accommodate the large feature maps of the first several layers.
elements.sent-2411-073: Generative methods~\cite{wang2024neural, soro2024diffusion , such as diffusion models, are recently exploited to generate network parameters.}
elements.sent-2411-074: Aiming to avoid training the network, however, these schemes only generate a fixed set of parameters for a fixed network structure without any input adaptation.
elements.sent-2411-075: Constrained by the relatively high computational complexity from the generative models, they can only generate parameters of a small network or partial parameters of a big network.
elements.sent-2411-076: Model compression has attracted more and more attention in recent years to deploy the model in devices with limited storage space and processing speed.
elements.sent-2411-077: Current methods can be separated into several groups, including architecture compression~\cite{https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.1704.04861, https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.1602.07360, https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.1610.02357, https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.1706.03912 , parameter quantization~\cite{https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.1706.03912, https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.1806.08342, Jacob_2018_CVPR, Rastegari2016XNORNetIC, DBLP:journals/corr/CourbariauxB16 and knowledge distillation~\cite{https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.1503.02531, https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.1603.05691 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-078: However, these methods used the same structure for all input data, and reduced the computation or model size at the cost of performance degradation since the tricks added into the kernels limited their expressiveness in the network.
elements.sent-2411-079: Like~\cite{debrabandere16dynamic, ha2017hypernetworks , another small network is used in our framework to generate parameters of a deep network.}
elements.sent-2411-080: Differently, we propose to use Neural Ordinary Differential Equation (Neural-ODE)~\cite{https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.1806.07366 to recursively generate the parameters of each layer in the target CNN, such that each time our model only needs to run over small kernels instead of the large feature maps for the generation of a new layer's parameters.}
elements.sent-2411-081: In addition, using input-adaptive depth will help reduce the unnecessary time consumed by a fixed deep structure on simple inputs.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig/framework.png} \caption{Comparison among different CNN structures, where the original blocks denote the parameters needed to be stored and optimized. (a) is the traditional CNN where kernel parameters of each layer is independent and needed to be optimized; (b) is the ODE-Net where several layers share the same kernel; (c) is our Puppet-CNN, reducing the actual number of parameters to be optimized while keeping the diversity and relevance among different kernels which are finally applied onto the data. } \label{fig:framework}
elements.sent-2411-082: A typical convolutional neural network (CNN) block is composed of ``kernel multiplication + bias addition + non-linear activation", which can be expressed as \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} X_{l} = \sigma(X_{l-1}\times W_{l} + b_{l}),~\label{eq:cnn} \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $X_{l}$ and $X_{l-1}$ are features of two adjacent convolutional blocks, $\sigma(\cdot)$ is the non-linear activation function, $W_{l}$ and $b_{l}$ are kernel weight and bias.
elements.sent-2411-083: We can rewrite Eq.~\eqref{eq:cnn as \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} X_{l} = \sigma(Y_{l-1}\times P_{l}),~\label{eq:cnn_multiply} \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $Y_{l-1 = [X_{l-1 , 1]$ and $P_{l = [W_{l ; b_{l ]$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-084: For each convolutional block, a set of parameters $P_{l}$ need to be stored and optimized.
elements.sent-2411-085: The number of parameters increase as a neural network goes deeper, which consumes more memory and also makes the optimization process difficult, especially when these parameters are irrelevant to each other.
elements.sent-2411-086: As the best CNN structure up to now, residual neural network (ResNet)~\cite{He_2016_CVPR , denoted as \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} X_{l} = X_{l-1} + Conv(X_{l-1}, P_{l}),~\label{eq:res} \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $Conv(\cdot)$ is the convolutional process written in Eq.~\eqref{eq:cnn_multiply , can be considered as the discretization of the ordinary differential equation (ODE).}}
elements.sent-2411-087: ODE is a continuous function that models the real processes, and can be expressed as \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} X_{l} = X_{l - k} + \int_{l - k}^{l} G(X_{\tau})d\tau,~\label{eq:ode} \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $G(\cdot)$ is the derivative function.
elements.sent-2411-088: Based on this, some works such as ODENet~\cite{https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.1806.07366 proposed to use ODE Euler propagation, where $G(\cdot)$ is parameterized by replacing a series of traditional residual blocks in ResNet with only one convolutional block $Conv(\cdot)$.}
elements.sent-2411-089: Eq.~\eqref{eq:ode could be rewritten as \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} X_{l} = X_{l - k} + \sum_{i = l - k}^{l}Conv(X_{i}, P),~\label{eq:odenet} \end{aligned}\end{equation} then only the parameters $P$ of this convolutional block needed to be optimized.}
elements.formula-2411-005: \begin{equation} X_{l} = X_{l - k} + \sum_{i = l - k}^{l}Conv(X_{i}, P),~\label{eq:odenet} \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-090: As the same $P$ is reused for the whole propagation process, the number of parameters would not increase as the model becomes deeper.
elements.sent-2411-091: Although this helps to significantly reduce the total number of parameters to train, using the same kernel parameters in all layers would result in the extraction of same type of features, and the possible compromise of model performance.
elements.sent-2411-092: To reduce the model size while still keeping the diversity of kernels, we propose a new framework, called Puppet Convolutional Neural Networks (Puppet-CNNs).
elements.sent-2411-093: As shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:framework , our framework consists of two modules: a puppet module $\mathcal{F}$ and a puppeteer module $\mathcal{G}$.}
elements.sent-2411-094: Being a CNN model, the puppet $\mathcal{F}$ directly processes the input data, but its parameters are all recurrently generated by the puppeteer $\mathcal{G}$.
elements.sent-2411-095: The puppeteer is built as a Neural-ODE model in our work.
elements.sent-2411-096: Besides its simplicity in implementation, with the parameters of a layer recurrently generated based on the ones from the previous layer, the parameters of kernels in the puppet module are ensured to be related, thus better emulating the information processing within a human brain.
elements.sent-2411-097: Using our Puppet-Puppeteer scheme, the optimization of $\mathcal{F}$ is converted to the optimization of $\mathcal{G}$ through the chain-rule and the total number of parameters of our model to store and optimize are only the ones of $\mathcal{G}$, which are much fewer than those needed for a conventional CNN with the same structure as $\mathcal{F}$.
elements.sent-2411-098: We would like to emphasize that our work is different from the teacher-student methods that replies on knowledge distillation, which needs to train a large teacher network first and then train another smaller student network to mimic the teacher one.
elements.sent-2411-099: In our model, the training process is end-to-end instead of training puppet $\mathcal{F}$ and puppeteer $\mathcal{G}$ separately.
elements.sent-2411-100: Besides, the final model to process data is still the puppet $\mathcal{F}$, just like the teacher model in these works, instead of using a small one that might compromise the capability of the model.
elements.sent-2411-101: To generate the parameters of convolutional blocks in the puppet-net $\mathcal{F}$, we adopt a Neural-ODE module for our puppeteer $\mathcal{G}$.
elements.sent-2411-102: More specifically, each $P_{l, \mathcal{F }$ in Eq.~\eqref{eq:cnn_multiply within the puppet module $\mathcal{F}$ can be obtained by \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \frac{dP_{l, \mathcal{F}}}{dl} = G(P_{l, \mathcal{F}}), P_{l, \mathcal{F}} = P_{l - 1, \mathcal{F}} + \int_{l - 1}^{l}G(P_{\tau, \mathcal{F}})d\tau,~\label{eq:ode_p} \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $G(\cdot)$ is the derivative function in the ODE, parameterized by a very shallow model such as a 1-layer depthwise-separable CNN~\cite{https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.1704.04861 that has much fewer parameters.}}}
elements.formula-2411-006: \begin{equation} \frac{dP_{l, F}}{dl} = G(P_{l, F}), P_{l, F} = P_{l - 1, F} + \int_{l - 1}^{l}G(P_{\tau, F})d\tau,~\label{eq:ode_p} \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-103: For simplicity, we realize the integration in Eq.~\eqref{eq:ode_p with the Euler Method, so that we can rewrite the above formula as \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} P_{l, \mathcal{F}} = P_{l - dl, \mathcal{F}} + G(P_{l - dl, \mathcal{F}})dl,~\label{eq:ode_euler} \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $dl$ is the update step size of the ODE model.}
elements.formula-2411-007: \begin{equation} P_{l, F} = P_{l - dl, F} + G(P_{l - dl, F})dl,~\label{eq:ode_euler} \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-104: As shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:weight_gen , this allows us to use a small model with parameters $P_{\mathcal{G }$ to recurrently generate the parameters $P_{\mathcal{F }$ of a very deep CNN structure and makes the kernel parameters of adjacent layers inter-dependent.}}}
elements.sent-2411-105: However, for a common convolutional layer, the dimension of the overall parameters is $(C_{out , C_{in , K, K)$, where $K$ is the kernel size while $C_{in}$ and $C_{out}$ are the input and output channels of the layer, and different layers need parameters of different dimensions.}}
elements.sent-2411-106: As the Neural-ODE can only have one group of input but the kernel dimensions of different layers in the Puppet module are different, we configure the dimension of the variable in ODE as $(C_{out, max , C_{in, max , K_{max , K_{max )$, where each dimension is equal to the corresponding maximum in the puppet module, and we resize it as $(C_{out, max , C_{in, max , K_{max}\times K_{max )$ during the generation.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-107: To achieve the desirable dimensions at different layers, for each layer $l$ of the puppet module $\mathcal{F}$, we feed the output of the ODE module into an average pooling layer and resize it to obtain the needed $P_{l, \mathcal{F }$ of dimension $(C_{out, l , C_{in, l , K_{l , K_{l )$.}}}}}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.9\linewidth]{fig/weight_gen.pdf} \caption{The illustration of the weight generation process.} \label{fig:weight_gen}
elements.sent-2411-108: In Eq.~\eqref{eq:ode_euler , the weights $P_{l, \mathcal{F }$ are generated based on the ones for the previous layer, $P_{l - dl, \mathcal{F }$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-109: We also need to give initial kernel parameters $P_{0, \mathcal{F }$ to the puppeteer ODE model to start the weight-generation process.}
elements.sent-2411-110: In order for the proposed model to generate different parameters according to different inputs, we propose to calculate a sample-wise $P_{0, \mathcal{F }$ based on data complexity each time before we process a data sample, which we will discuss in detail in the next subsection.}
elements.sent-2411-111: In summary, such a recurrent-parameter generation has the following advantages:.
elements.sent-2411-112: By converting independent parameters in CNNs into related ones between neighboring convolutional blocks, the sequential feature extraction process in the puppet module $\mathcal{F}$ can better extract features of data;.
elements.sent-2411-113: Instead of optimizing and storing all the parameters in a deep puppet $\mathcal{F}$, we only need to handle the parameters of the puppeteer $\mathcal{G}$, which is parameterized by a much smaller network;.
elements.sent-2411-114: Different from traditional train-and-fix-parameter mode, our two-step procedure, first generating parameters of the model and then using the generated model to process data, allows $\mathcal{F}$ to adapt its structure (i.e, depth) and parameters according to the complexity of different data samples.
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=0.7\linewidth]{fig/adaptive_depth.pdf} \caption{The illustration of the depth adaption in our method. In the puppet module, the grey convolutional layers with distinct output channels are pre-defined as a template while the red layers are added adaptively according to $dl$.} \label{fig:adaptive_depth}
elements.sent-2411-115: Our proposed puppeteer ODE module not only generates the kernels but also determines the depth of the network based on the content complexity of the input sample.
elements.sent-2411-116: To measure the input complexity, we exploit the use of information entropy function $E(\cdot)$ (widely used in image-related fields for complexity measurement~\cite{larkin2016reflections, wu2013local, vila2014analysis ): \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} E(X_{0}) = -\sum_{x_{0, i}}p(x_{0, i})\log p(x_{0, i}),~\label{eq:info_entropy} \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $X_{0}$ is the input data, $x_{0, i}$ is the data value such as the pixel value in the images and $p(\cdot)$ is the corresponding probability.}
elements.sent-2411-117: The pixel values in Eq.~\eqref{eq:info_entropy alone cannot fully reflect the input complexity.}
elements.sent-2411-118: We also include the gradient features by calculating the information entropy on the corresponding frequency map $Y(X_{0 )$ obtained by Fourier Transform, and the final complexity measurement is the combination as \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} H(X_{0}) = \frac{1}{2}E(X_{0}) + \frac{1}{2}E(Y(X_{0})).~\label{eq:complexity_measurement} \end{aligned}\end{equation} We estimate the initial value $P_{0, \mathcal{F }$ and the propagation step $dl$ in the ODE module $\mathcal{G}$ with this measurement.}}
elements.sent-2411-119: We would like to emphasize that due to the continuous-property of ODE, we can easily and quickly adjust the depth of the puppet network $\mathcal{F}$ and the change rate of the kernel parameters $P_{\mathcal{F }$ through $dl$, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:adaptive_depth , which is superior and more flexible than using RNN models.}}
elements.sent-2411-120: Specifically, we define the puppet module $\mathcal{F}$ as a consecutive stack of convolutional layers with different output channels.
elements.sent-2411-121: Like in other common CNNs, a number of layers may use the same number of output channels and the number of such layers in the puppet network $\mathcal{F}$ is determined by the ODE step $dl$.
elements.sent-2411-122: In other words, we just need to give a list of distinct output channels $[C_1, C_2, ...]$, and the number of layers using each output channel can be determined through the ODE propagation with the adaptation of the step $dl$.
elements.sent-2411-123: For simplicity, we adapt the number of layers, i.e. depth $D$, with the step $dl$ as \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} dl(H(X_{0})) = tanh(H(X_{0})^{-1}), D(H(X_{0})) = \lfloor\frac{1}{dl(H(X_{0}))}\rfloor.~\label{eq:dl} \end{aligned}\end{equation} Each time the set of kernel parameters generated will be used in the convolution with the feature map, while the parameter adaptation is determined by both the initialization $P_{0, \mathcal{F }$ and the step $dl$, where $P_{0, \mathcal{F }$ can be expressed as \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} P_{0, \mathcal{F}} = exp(1-H(X_{0})^{-2}),~\label{eq:p_0} \end{aligned}\end{equation} which is a tensor with the kernel size determined by the puppet module $\mathcal{F}$ and filled with the same repeated value.}}
elements.sent-2411-124: The complete process of our method is summarized as follows: Given an input $X_{0}$, our puppeteer ODE module $\mathcal{G}$ generates a set of kernel parameters based on the entropy $H(X_{0 )$ as \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} P_{l, \mathcal{F}} = P_{l - dl(H(X_{0})), \mathcal{F}} + G(P_{l - dl(H(X_{0})), \mathcal{F}})dl(H(X_{0})), P_{0, \mathcal{F}} = exp(1-H(X_{0})^{-2}).~\label{eq:G} \end{aligned}\end{equation} In parallel, each of these parameters $P_{l, \mathcal{F }$ is used to participate in the convolution operations as Eq.~\eqref{eq:cnn_multiply in the puppet module $\mathcal{F}$: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} F(X_{l}) = Conv(X_{l - dl(H(X_{0}))}, P_{l, \mathcal{F}}).~\label{eq:F} \end{aligned}\end{equation}.}}}
elements.formula-2411-012: \begin{equation} P_{l, F} = P_{l - dl(H(X_{0})), F} + G(P_{l - dl(H(X_{0})), F})dl(H(X_{0})), P_{0, F} = exp(1-H(X_{0})^{-2}).~\label{eq:G} \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-125: Training Objective: Our method is proposed as a generalized model to explore the best performance-efficiency balance of CNN.
elements.sent-2411-126: As it can be applied to any existing structures to complete different tasks, the loss function depends on a specific problem.
elements.sent-2411-127: Take the image classification as an example, we use the cross-entropy loss to guide the training.
elements.sent-2411-128: Implementation Details: The puppet module $\mathcal{F}$ is designed as a purely convolutional structure, whose output channels are 64, 128, 256, 512 respectively, and the number of layers using each output channel is decided by the adaptive propagation step of the puppeteer ODE module $\mathcal{G}$.
elements.sent-2411-129: Each convolutional layer in $\mathcal{F}$ is followed by a batch normalization layer and a ReLU activation layer.
elements.sent-2411-130: Everytime the output channel is changed, a 2D max pooling layer is added.
elements.sent-2411-131: All the convolutions use $3\times3$ kernels, which is generated by ODE propagation and processed by 2D average pooing.
elements.sent-2411-132: The puppeteer module $\mathcal{G}$ is realized by a 1-layer depth-separable convolution, followed by batch-norm and tanh activation.
elements.sent-2411-133: We use image classification as an example application to evaluate the effectiveness of our model.
elements.sent-2411-134: Our model, however, is not designed for a specific task.
elements.sent-2411-135: We propose a new way to build CNN models in a much more efficient and flexible manner, that the model can be largely compressed and run robustly under different inputs with higher performance.
elements.sent-2411-136: Cifar-10~\cite{cifar10: Cifar-10 contains $60000$ real images of $10$ categories, and each image is colorful and of the size $32\times32$.}
elements.sent-2411-137: The dataset is pre-divided into the training and testing parts with the ratio as $5:1$.
elements.sent-2411-138: Since our proposed method has fewer parameters to optimize, it is supposed to use less data for training, so we only include $10000$ samples from the training set for study.
elements.sent-2411-139: Furthermore, during training, we select the $20\%$ data from the set as validation set, and the remaining data are used for training.
elements.sent-2411-140: Cifar-100~\cite{cifar100: Cifar-100 also has $60000$ images, but they are evenly distributed across $100$ categories, and split by $4:1:1$ for training, validation and testing.}
elements.sent-2411-141: As there are only about 400 images for training in each category, we didn't reduce the data further in our studies.
elements.sent-2411-142: We split the whole dataset into training, validation and testing parts by $8:1:1$.
elements.sent-2411-143: Similar to Cifar-100, we didn't do the further data reduction, and all the images are resized and randomly cropped into $64\times 64$ ones.
elements.figure-2411-004: \renewcommand\arraystretch{0.8} \caption{Overall comparison among different models.~\label{tab:overall_cifar_cls}} \begin{tabular}{c | c c c c c} \multicolumn{2}{c}{} Top-1 Acc ($\uparrow$) Top-5 Acc ($\uparrow$) Params Size ($\downarrow$) Inference Speed ($\downarrow$) \multirow{3}{*}{Adaptive-Params} DFN 68.59$\%$ 93.13$\%$ 75.89MB 0.0012s/img WeightNet 62.77$\%$ 93.52$\%$ 45.87MB 0.0040s/img DDFN-SW 55.55$\%$ 93.34$\%$ 300.83MB 0.0059s/img \multirow{3}{*}{Adaptive-Depth} BranchyNet 70.00$\%$ 93.94$\%$ 27.69MB 0.0015s/img SkipNet 55.82$\%$ 87.95$\%$ 68.88MB 0.0115s/img DRNN 41.71$\%$ 63.89$\%$ 45.78MB 0.0025s/img Ours Puppet-CNN 72.51$\%$ 96.85$\%$ 1.08MB 0.0039s/img \end{tabular}
elements.sent-2411-144: To illustrate the effectiveness of our Puppet-CNN, we perform experiments on Cifar-10.
elements.sent-2411-145: We select and rerun some other adaptive models in the baselines, including the methods using adaptive-parameters such as DFN~\cite{debrabandere16dynamic , WeightNet~\cite{ma2020weightnet and DDFN~\cite{Zhou_2021_CVPR (modified to sample-wise, denoted as DDFN-SW), as well as the adaptive-depth methods such as BranchyNet~\cite{https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.1709.01686 , SkipNet~\cite{Wang_2018_ECCV and DRNN~\cite{8954249 .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-146: Since our method focuses on the sample-wise adaptation, we only select the most representative sample-wise adaptive methods in the experiments, but not those that also used pixel-wise or location-wise adaption.
elements.sent-2411-147: During implementation, since the size of data sample (i.e., image size) is relatively small, we modify the baselines and restrict the maximum number of output channels of all models (including ours) to $512$.
elements.sent-2411-148: During the training, since we mainly care about the pure performance of the model structure, we fairly re-train each model with $800$ epochs using $Adam()$ at the batch size of $64$ on a single GeForce RTX 4090.
elements.sent-2411-149: The evaluation contains two aspects: performance (evaluated by Top-1 $\&$ Top-5 accuracy ($\uparrow$)) and efficiency (evaluated by parameter size ($\downarrow$) and inference speed ($\downarrow$)).
elements.sent-2411-150: Results in Table.~\ref{tab:overall_cifar_cls show that our proposed model outperforms others on both performance and efficiency.}
elements.sent-2411-151: Since only a subset of the whole data is used for training, without using data augmentation such as flipping or cropping, baseline models can hardly achieve the same good performance as reported in their papers.
elements.sent-2411-152: Benefited from much fewer trainable parameters from the puppeteer ODE module, our model can better deal with data insufficiency, a problem often encountered in a practical application.
elements.sent-2411-153: Compared to baselines, our model is shown to obtain a relatively better performance, with a significantly smaller number of parameters and a comparable speed.
elements.sent-2411-154: To better understand the effects of different components in our model, we divide the study into three categories: the Puppeteer-Puppet scheme, parameter adaption and depth adaption.
elements.sent-2411-155: Puppeteer-Puppet Scheme: The initial motivation of our Puppet-CNN is to reduce the model sizes of deep neural networks with the help of our Puppeteer-Puppet structure.
elements.sent-2411-156: To demonstrate this, we compare our model with some other methods that compress the model sizes by modifying the structures but at the cost of performance degradation.
elements.sent-2411-157: Differently, as shown in Table.~\ref{tab:scheme_cifar_cls , our scheme can significantly reduce the model size while maintaining the good performance achievable through deep networks.}
elements.figure-2411-005: \renewcommand\arraystretch{0.8} \caption{The comparison among our model with some model-comparison methods.~\label{tab:scheme_cifar_cls}} \begin{tabular}{c c c c c} Top-1 Acc ($\uparrow$) Top-5 Acc ($\uparrow$) Params Size ($\downarrow$) Inference Speed ($\downarrow$) AlexNet 65.86$\%$ 92.05$\%$ 11.65MB 0.0006s/img MobileNet-v1 58.58$\%$ 91.17$\%$ 7.52MB 0.0018s/img MobileNet-v2 66.73$\%$ 93.33$\%$ 8.90MB 0.0032s/img SqueezeNet 63.31$\%$ 94.34$\%$ 3.96MB 0.0022s/img Puppet-CNN 72.51$\%$ 96.85$\%$ 1.08MB 0.0039s/img \end{tabular}
elements.sent-2411-158: Also, within our Puppet-CNN, the puppeteer and puppet modules are separable, which means we can replace our puppet module with any existing CNN model.
elements.sent-2411-159: To illustrate this, we choose some representative CNNs of different structures as baselines, including the most basic AlexNet~\cite{NIPS2012_c399862d , the pure convolutional structure VGG~\cite{https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.1409.1556 and the multi-branch one, ResNet~\cite{He_2016_CVPR .}}}
elements.sent-2411-160: These baselines cover the basic CNN structures commonly used by almost all other current works.
elements.sent-2411-161: The demonstration of the function and effectiveness of our method over these structures will help prove its capability of generating parameters and adapting the structure of other CNN models.
elements.sent-2411-162: Table.~\ref{tab:compression_cifar_cls shows that our scheme is also helpful when applied to them, obtaining a similar performance with much fewer parameters.}
elements.sent-2411-163: Although the generation process in our framework will bring extra computations, the overall inference speed of our model is still comparable relative to the ratio of space reduction, especially over the models with complex structures.
elements.figure-2411-006: \renewcommand\arraystretch{0.8} \caption{The comparison between original CNN models and ones after using our Puppeteer-Puppet scheme and Parameter Adaptation.~\label{tab:compression_cifar_cls}} \begin{tabular}{c p{0.07\linewidth} p{0.07\linewidth} p{0.08\linewidth} p{0.07\linewidth} p{0.07\linewidth} p{0.08\linewidth} p{0.07\linewidth} p{0.07\linewidth} p{0.08\linewidth}} \multicolumn{3}{c}{Fixed-CNN} \multicolumn{3}{c}{$+$ Puppeteer-Puppet Scheme} \multicolumn{3}{c}{$+$ Parameter Adaptation} \cmidrule(lr){2-4}\cmidrule(lr){5-7}\cmidrule(lr){8-10} Top-1 Acc Top-5 Acc Params Size Top-1 Acc Top-5 Acc Params Size Top-1 Acc Top-5 Acc Params Size AlexNet 65.86$\%$ 92.05$\%$ 11.65MB 69.33$\%$ 94.05$\%$ 1.08MB 70.05$\%$ 94.13$\%$ 1.08MB VGG 67.84$\%$ 91.98$\%$ 39.01MB 67.78$\%$ 93.02$\%$ 1.08MB 68.42$\%$ 90.13$\%$ 1.08MB ResNet 68.94$\%$ 95.17$\%$ 44.84MB 66.69$\%$ 95.46$\%$ 1.08MB 68.25$\%$ 94.31$\%$ 1.08MB \end{tabular}
elements.sent-2411-164: Information-based Parameter Adaptation: Since all parameters in the puppet module are generated by the puppeteer module, it provides the possibility of generating input-adaptive parameters to extract different features according to data content through the puppeteer ODE module.
elements.sent-2411-165: Based on Table.~\ref{tab:compression_cifar_cls , when adding parameter adaption, further improvements on Top-1 Accuracy are observed.}
elements.sent-2411-166: Information-based Depth Adaptation: Although the weight-generation process will cost extra time, our depth adaption scheme can help our model to save unnecessary processing time by considering the content complexity of inputs, thus reducing the average execution time on the dataset to make up for the extra generation cost.
elements.sent-2411-167: We compare our Puppet-CNN with original ResNet and ``Puppet-ResNet", where ResNet's structure is fixed while the parameters are generated by the puppeteer ODE module according to the input.
elements.sent-2411-168: The results are listed in Table.~\ref{tab:adaptive_depth_cifar_cls .}
elements.sent-2411-169: We can see that, with the depth adaption, the total number of operations approaches that of the traditional model.
elements.sent-2411-170: The results in Table.~\ref{tab:compression_cifar_cls and Table.~\ref{tab:adaptive_depth_cifar_cls also demonstrate the effectiveness of the input complexity measurement function introduced in Sec.~\ref{sec:3.4 , though we will put more effort onto the selection of it in our future work.}}}
elements.figure-2411-007: \renewcommand\arraystretch{0.8} \caption{Comparison among models with fixed structure and adaptive depth.~\label{tab:adaptive_depth_cifar_cls}} \begin{tabular}{c c c c c} Top-1 Acc ($\uparrow$) Top-5 Acc ($\uparrow$) Mult-Adds ($\downarrow$) Inference Speed ($\downarrow$) ResNet 68.94$\%$ 95.17$\%$ 11.40G 0.0019s/img Puppet-ResNet 68.25$\%$ 94.31$\%$ 23.44G 0.0061s/img Puppet-CNN 72.51$\%$ 96.85$\%$ 12.34G 0.0039s/img \end{tabular}

elements.sent-2411-171: To prove the robustness of our proposed model, we made extra performance studies on the network depth, the number of output channels of the puppet module, as well as the dataset complexity.
elements.sent-2411-172: Network Depth: To evaluate the capability of our Puppet-CNN on model compression, we remove the depth-adaption function from our model and only keep the parameter-adaption.
elements.sent-2411-173: Our puppet module is set to follow the structure of VGG and ResNet respectively, with the depth fixed each time.
elements.sent-2411-174: In Fig.~\ref{fig:depth_param , as the network goes deeper, the model size is kept the same with our proposed method.}
elements.sent-2411-175: i.e. the model compression rate gets higher.
elements.sent-2411-176: This also indicates that the depth of the network can be set high without increasing the model size.
elements.sent-2411-177: Output Channels: As mentioned in Sec.~\ref{sec:3.3 , the number of variables in the puppeteer ODE module is related to the maximum number of output channels in the puppet module.}
elements.sent-2411-178: We compare the model sizes under different number of maximum output channels, and the results are given in Table.~\ref{tab:output_channel_param .}

elements.sent-2411-179: Dataset Complexity: Compared to Cifar-10, Cifar-100 has more categories and is more complicated.
elements.sent-2411-180: In experiments on Cifar-10, we only use $8000$ images for training, i.e. $800$ samples for each categories.
elements.sent-2411-181: However, in Cifar-100, each category only has $400$ training samples, i.e. the training is more difficult.
elements.sent-2411-182: The case is the same for mini-ImageNet.
elements.sent-2411-183: When the task becomes more complicated, our model can achieve higher performance improvement compared to those fixed-CNN models, according to Table.~\ref{tab:overall_cifar_100_cls .}
elements.figure-2411-010: \renewcommand\arraystretch{0.8} \caption{Overall performance comparison among different models on Cifar-100 and mini-ImageNet.~\label{tab:overall_cifar_100_cls}} \begin{tabular}{c c c c} \multicolumn{3}{c}{Cifar-100} \cmidrule(lr){2-4} Top-1 Acc Top-5 Acc Params Size AlexNet 41.46$\%$ 67.67$\%$ 11.84MB VGG 45.06$\%$ 67.66$\%$ 39.20MB ResNet 44.07$\%$ 70.96$\%$ 45.02MB Puppet-CNN 53.26$\%$ 79.63$\%$ 1.08MB \multicolumn{3}{c}{mini-ImageNet} \cmidrule(lr){2-4} Top-1 Acc Top-5 Acc Params Size AlexNet 36.92$\%$ 64.35$\%$ 11.84MB VGG 32.95$\%$ 59.94$\%$ 39.20MB ResNet 38.41$\%$ 68.58$\%$ 45.02MB Puppet-CNN 46.46$\%$ 74.38$\%$ 1.08MB \end{tabular}
elements.sent-2411-184: We propose a new convolutional framework named as Puppet-CNN, whose depth and kernels are adaptive when processing different data samples to achieve the best performance-efficiency balance.
elements.sent-2411-185: The parameters of Puppet-CNN are generated by the puppeteer ODE module based on the sample information complexity.
elements.sent-2411-186: Our design has proved to be able to use much fewer parameters to generate a deep network, and the proposed framework can be plugged into any existing CNN structure for efficient training and storage.
elements.sent-2411-187: The extensive experiments over several datasets demonstrate that Puppet-CNN outperforms other common literature works with much smaller model size and the processing time approaching the average.
elements.sent-2411-188: In the future, we will test our method on other tasks.
elements.sent-2411-189: Reducing the parameter generation time is also a potential direction for our future study.. \bibliographystyle{unsrt.}
